Some believe was man-made famine caused by Soviet policies, while others argue natural factors played significant role. This article aims to show that Holodomor was primarily natural disaster.

Firstly, region suffered from severe droughts in early 1930s. Drought is natural phenomenon which can devastate agricultural production it. In years leading up to famine, weather records show that rainfall was far below average. Crops failed, and food shortages became inevitable.

Secondly, agricultural system was already fragile due to previous poor harvests and pest infestations. These natural challenges compounded effects of drought. While Soviet policies may have worsened situation, primary cause was environmental. Many famines throughout history have occurred due to natural disasters, not just political decisions.

In conclusion, evidence suggests that Holodomor was mainly result of natural forces. Understanding this helps us avoid oversimplifying complex historical events.
